summary: Removed the in-repo Rust `tools/dadosod` subtree and disconnected it from the workspace and dump-asm packaging path. The PR deleted the dadosod sources, license, README, Cargo manifest, workspace membership, Docker build stage, dump-asm invocation, and stale Cargo.lock dependencies. There was no PR body or review discussion in the slice, so the reason is inferred only from the title and diff: housekeeping to stop building and running dadosod as part of the repository tooling.
what_changed: Deleted the entire `tools/dadosod` subtree, including Rust source files, README, GPL-3.0 license file, package manifest, and local .gitignore.
Removed `tools/dadosod` from the root Cargo workspace `members` list.
Removed the Docker `build-rust` stage that copied `tools/dadosod` and ran `cargo build -r`.
Stopped copying the built `dadosod` binary into the `dump-asm` image.
Removed the `dadosod dol build/ssbm.us.1.2/main.dol -m build/map.csv -o /output` call from `.github/packages/dump-asm/entrypoint.sh`.
Cleaned Cargo.lock entries for dadosod-only dependencies, including `argh`, `csv`, `dol`, `ppc750cl`, `thiserror`, `bincode`, `ryu`, `itoa`, and related transitive packages.
Cargo.lock also updated `regex-automata` from 0.4.4 to 0.4.5; the slice does not explain whether that was intentional or an incidental lockfile refresh.
smart_moves: Removal was complete across source, workspace registration, lockfile, Docker build, image copy, and runtime invocation, avoiding dangling references to a deleted tool.
The Dockerfile cleanup eliminated an entire Rust build stage for dump-asm images, likely reducing CI/package build work, though no performance numbers are provided.
The dump-asm entrypoint was updated alongside the packaging changes, so generated dump outputs no longer depend on a binary that is no longer installed.
Cargo.lock cleanup removed obsolete git dependencies on external PowerPC/DOL crates that were only used by dadosod.
decomp_lessons: When retiring an auxiliary decompilation tool, remove it from every integration point: workspace manifest, lockfile, CI/container build stages, install/copy steps, and scripts that invoke it.
Tool subtree deletion should include generated packaging assumptions, not just source files, because CI images can retain hidden dependencies on removed binaries.
Lockfile churn can expose whether a deleted tool was carrying extra third-party or git dependencies; cleaning those entries keeps the workspace dependency graph easier to audit.
For future tooling removals, document why the tool is obsolete if possible; this slice has no PR body or comments, so future readers can only infer motivation from the diff.
naming_conventions: The removed tool was named `dadosod` as both the Cargo package and installed CLI binary.
The removed source layout used Rust module names `doldisasm`, `tracker`, `symbol`, and `utils` under `tools/dadosod/src/`.
No new decomp symbol, function, struct, or header naming conventions were introduced.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No assembly matching tactics were present; this was a tooling/housekeeping PR.

The removed dump-asm script step had used `dadosod dol` on `build/ssbm.us.1.2/main.dol` with `build/map.csv` output to `/output`, indicating dadosod had been part of the DOL dump/disassembly artifact generation path.
The retained dump-asm steps in the excerpt still run `tools/parse_map.py` and `tools/m2ctx/m2ctx.py -pn`, but the dadosod-generated output was dropped.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, reviews, or PR body text were present in the provided slice.
There is no evidence of reviewer-requested changes or rationale beyond the title and diff.
follow_up_queries: Why was dadosod removed from the melee repository?
Was dadosod replaced by another dump-asm or disassembly artifact generator?
Which PR introduced the dadosod subtree and dump-asm invocation?
Did removing dadosod change the contents of dump-asm package outputs?
Was the regex-automata Cargo.lock update intentional or incidental?
